name: explicit_strength_reduction
title: Spell out strength-reduction sequence in C to override GCC's default starting multiplier
summary:
GCC 2.7.2 reduces `r5 * 0x2A38` starting from `r5 * 2`, but target uses `r5 * 16` as the starting point. The two reductions produce different shift/add sequences. Writing the natural multiplication forces the wrong path.

Fix: spell the reduction out so GCC has nothing to optimize:
```c
int t = (r5 << 4) + r8;     /* r5*16 + r8 */
t = (t << 6) + r8;          /* (r5*16 + r8)*64 + r8 */
t = (t << 3) - t;           /* * 7 */
```
This forces the reduction starting point to match target.
proof: func_80048864 (commit cda948e/ce5237d) was stuck for 164min on first attempt with default `r5 * 0x2A38`; matched in the deep retry by spelling out the reduction sequence explicitly.

structural: target asm contains a chain of `sll`/`addu`/`subu` that doesn't start from `addu $X, $r5, $r5` (which would be the GCC default *2 starting point)
C source uses `r5 * <large_constant>` where the constant has a specific factorization GCC won't find naturally

rule_skeleton.instruction: Replace `r5 * <const>` with explicit shift/add chain. Decompose <const> manually starting from the target's first multiplier (e.g., 16 instead of GCC's default 2).

notes: Find target's reduction starting point by reading the first `sll` after the constant load: `sll $X, $r5, N` means starting multiplier is 2^N.
Then factor your constant accordingly: 0x2A38 = 16 * 2 * 256 + 24, etc. Spell each step.
Workers tend to over-trust GCC; explicit reduction is one of the few reliable overrides.
